text: The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position. Upon initial exposure, a large diverticulum is identified in the surgical field.

The surgeon begins bisection from the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon, proceeding in a medial to lateral direction. The proximal part of the superior rectal vessels is identified and ligated, while the inferior mesenteric artery and vein are left intact due to the benign nature of the disease.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection over the Toldt's fascia, exposing retroperitoneal structures. Lateral attachments are released, and the sigmoid mesocolon is mobilized and dissected along the embryological planes.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the left ureter and gonadal vessels, along with the left common iliac artery. The distal part of the superior rectal vessels is identified, ligated, and divided.
During the lateral dissection, the surgeon exposes and identifies the left ureter. The left mesocolon is mobilized through the taut fascia.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal dissection of the mesocolon up to the presumed proximal transection site. The upper rectum is transected using a robotic stapler, and the colon is exteriorized through the suprapubic incision in preparation for anastomosis.
The surgeon places the anvil and uses a circular 31mm stapler to create an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis, completing the final step of the procedure.
